other_observers: 4 particpants on Victor Emanuel Nature Tours tour;
specific_location: About 0.5 mi southeast of "public" Lesser Prairie-Chicken lek and blind
description: One male seen was identical to Western Meadowlark with the following differences: 1. malar region was distinctly ENTIRELY yellow. 2. cheek was very sparsely streaked. 3. tail was extensively white, with outer two rectrices (r5, r6) entirely white, r4 being mostly white with razor thin line of brown along outer margin of outer web at tip about 1/4 in to 1/8 in in length, and with r3 being mostly white with fairly broad, squared off, rectangular patch of brown along outer web for about one-half in at tip of rectrix; 4. Back, covert, and tertial feathers apparently paler with narrower bars. Also, by analyzing photos, I have found that the rump feathers appear to have dark centers on Easterns, pale centers on the Westerns.Behaviors: Males singing from perches (e.g., yucca stalks) in grassland. Females carrying food quietly, seen when perched on yucca stalks or shrub tops.Call: Two males singing continuously, heard in different directions at the same time. Song was invariably a high, fluty, "Wheeee Seee-ooo seee". Did not hear call notes from either bird. Song very different from staccato, warbling, songs of Western Meadowlark.Plumage: breeding plumage
